speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Mrs. Fela Abramowicz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we crossed the Alps on foot, and we went-- eventually reached the-- the-- the Austrian town of Graz, which is northern Austria. And they took us from there to what used to be a camp, some sort of military camp, or something, a place called [? Sladnietz. ?] And there we stayed for three months, until, that-- as you see, we had to register everywhere for-- for refugee. We registered there in [? Sladnietz ?] as refugees. And then they took us to the border of Austria and Italy, where you had to cross through some sort of, not through the water, but through something that's connected with the-- with the land because it's a peninsula. And that put-- put us in [INAUDIBLE]. And we went through to, I don't say what happens on the foots to the Italian with my daughter. They caught us, and they brought us back. That's not important. We reached Italy. We reached Italy in November '45. And then they took us to a camp in Cremona. It's northern Italy. It was big camp, a lot of refugees, not just Jewish, but from everywhere. Europe was-- a lot of people were displaced. Displaced people's camp, and we felt-- felt like we were back in a camp. And we had food and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went-- we went out and bought some nice things to wear, who had a bit of money, you know? And it was nice to be in a nice country, lovely climate, nice-- nice shops, nice things, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't like the feeling of being in a camp again. So we-- we-- our group, we had a lot of people who were with us, who-- who were together in the-- in the partisans. So we put together a group, who went to the authorities, to the refugee authorities to the-- at this time, it was called, IRO, or I-R-O. And also the Jewish relief agency from America, the Joint, which took us under their-- they helped us. And we thought we-- we'll ask them to take us out of the camp and put us in a smaller place, so we can all be together, and pool our resources, and establish a kibbutz-- a kibbutz, sort of, you know? So we won't have to feel like we're being in a camp, and we will do our own thing. So they agreed. And they-- they rented us a villa in another place called, Reggio Emilia. And we stayed there for a short time. Some of us went down to-- to the Aliyah Bet, the illegal migration, the ones without children, or the younger people. They departed on a ship, which was later all caught, but I'm not talking about this. It's a different story. Something like the exodus ship that was in La Spezia. It was all documented in the thing, in the history of the-- but I won't talk about this. I wasn't in it. But half of her kibbutz, ex-partisans went there. And eventually, they were taken away to the Cypresses, and later on, let into Palestine. It was still Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on, the [INAUDIBLE] said they need this place for-- for the illegal Aliyah, for the younger people because it was close to a port and close to the sea and everything. So we had to go somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us-- there was another partisan, ex-partisan kibbutz established somewhere in the mountains, on top of the Apennines. So we were sent there. It was a very beautiful place, a lovely mountain, all surroundings, and everything was very comfortable. It used to be a-- it was a place called [? Accra ?] [? Santa, ?] which means, holy water. So they had some baths there before the war. And Mussolini used to send his young people there, and that's why they built this big place. So we occupied it. It had a big kitchen. And we were there for-- for a while. And all the time, we were there, they're talking about migrating, you know, to Palestine, you know? And-- and we-- we couldn't get-- some people, parts of our group were taken, the ones without children, or the ones, single people, you know. But they didn't want to touch the people with the children. And by the way, in this-- in this partisan Otriad, there were 20 woman pregnant at the same time. Because after-- after the war, all the Jewish family, and new couples were established. And everybody desperate to start a family, because everybody lost all their families. So, you know? So-- and then we couldn't climb every time the- the mountains. Every time we had to go out to buy something, we had to climb for-- for supplies. Having-- had to climb the mountains up and down, up and down. So we went to the organizations, to the refugee organization and said, we can't stand anymore. It's too hard on the pregnant women and the women with the babies to climb all those mountains. So give us somewhere else. We thought where they don't have to climb? So we got into a place next to the-- on the sea, resort. So we went from the mountains to the sea. And we ended up in Anzio, a famous-- a famous place in the-- in the war, where the Americans had-- it was a big battle and American had many losses. It was a military cemetery there. About 3,000 or something, or 4,000 Americans in this battle perished. And I think they made a film about this battle, too. So that's where we were, in one of the villas there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a resort, so a lot of people close around. So a lot of people from Rome used to go there for their-- I think, for their holidays. I think in the ancient day, it was called Antium, where Nero went for his-- there was still grottos and everything. Very beautiful place. Our windows, you could see the sea. Very beautiful. This was the Tyrrhenian Sea, part of the Mediterranean, but it was called Tyrrhenian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we were close to the Adriatic, on the other side. So we went south of Rome, to this place, Anzio. And we stayed there till-- from-- what from-- from when? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '46, I think? It was already '46, '47 to '48, or a little more. We stayed there more than a year. But this time, because I-- I have to-- sorry, more than a year. It was 1 and 1/2 years we stayed there in Anzio.

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time, I got pregnant and gave birth to my son. He was born in Anzio. Anzio, Nettuno. It was a twin town. There was the hospital, and so I had to go there. And after that, there-- there was the proclamation of the State of Israel. So migration to Israel became possible, and a lot of people departed. And the kibbutz started getting empty, empty and emptier. And my sister, this time, arrived-- was migrating to Australia. She was living in Paris, one of my sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was migrating to Australia. So she said, don't go to to Israel, because it's a new country. You've been through so much, and you have three children. How can you? How will you be able to manage? And there's a war there. You know, there was the war for independence. I said, but I'm determined to go. I'm a Zionist. She said, first come here, have a rest. And then when you're ready, you can go-- you can go to-- to Israel. So I talk with my husband. Reluctantly, he-- he-- he agreed to go. So we started making arrangements to go to-- to-- to Australia. By this time, half of the kibbutz where we stayed, and half of another kibbutz empty because everybody was already went, migrated. And so we decided to join to the two together again. But this time, we went to-- closer terrain, to a place called Castel Gandolfo, which was a place where the Pope has his summer residence. We were next door to the Pope. Not this Pope. The-- the Pope that-- what went through the war, you know? I think it was Pious the XII, or something. It was the last Pious, I think. And when did you eventually leave Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, yeah, this was a matter of waiting, in this stage. And my son was around 16 months-- 18 months when we came to Castel Gandolfo. When we left-- almost 18 months. When we left, when he was 18 months, so how long was this? So it was-- it was summer, I think. We-- we left in-- in August, we left for Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed about six months, I would say, close to six months in Castel Gandolfo. This was just a method of waiting for migration, you know? So-- And what-- what-- what date did you come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Australia the 17th of September, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was-- this was one week before my son's second birthday. He was the 24th of September. And we arrived on the 17th of September. So we-- we celebrated 47 years in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder if there is anything that you would like to say either to your children, or your grandchildren, any message that you might have about your life and your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what can I say? I said, thanks God for the partisans, because when they were showing the film, I said go home and look at the film. Because not of this, you wouldn't-- you wouldn't all be here. So, you know, I mean, those people who survived and tried to escape and did something about it, eventually we have our generation, two generations. So you know, we survived, and continue to new generations, two new generations came since then, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And doing well, you know? I mean, you wouldn't believe that somebody-- somebody born in-- like, in those circumstances will come out alive, and well, and well-adjusted. I didn't believe it myself. I couldn't believe my luck, you know? So two very bright children, who got the best education, become professional people, and bring up their children, in a-- in the best way as possible. So we are very, how do you say it? When someone's-- I forgot. Resilient people. Jewish people are very resilient, and very resourceful. And they always-- if they make an effort, they'll always survive. Thank you very much. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Would you please introduce your son, Mrs. Abramowicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my son, Sam Abrahams. Originally, Zelman Abramowicz, but he prefers to be called by that other name. I'm very, very proud of him. And he's-- what can you say? The-- the love of my life. And he's got a nice family, who give me immense pleasure, a charming wife, and two lovely sons, Michael and Simon. And well, I feel secure with him. He's-- he my security. He always-- he's always there when I need him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say a few words about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother's quite a remarkable woman. She's come through incredible traumas from an age younger than my older son is now. When I compare what she's lived through at that age, and what my older son, who's never had to really endure any suffering, of any kind, it makes me realize what an incredible achievement it is for my mother, not only to have survived what she went through, but to have come through and be-- and live a normal life. She's been able to extract happiness, comfort, and security, from her environment, and it's really an amazing thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about much of your parents' wartime experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I know a lot about it now. When I was little, I guess all young Jewish kids knew about the Holocaust. But not really in any detail, only in general terms, that these things happened. And I guess like all kids, you don't really think about it much. You just get on with playing you games, and going to school, and being as naughty as you can manage to get away with. And-- but as-- as I grew older, I guess, I heard more of the stories from both my parents. I became aware in greater detail of their history through the war years, and-- and how they came to get out of the ghettos, and out of Poland, and ultimately found themselves in Italy after tramping around half of Europe, and-- and eventually making their way to Australia. And that as the greater detail arose, I guess I-- I became more conscious of those aspects of being a Jew, and-- and of being an Australian, for that matter, and living in a country like we live in, that I wouldn't otherwise have been without those stories. So they're an important, very important part of my upbringing. And I've in fact, tried to have my children know about those things as well. And hopefully, they will have some understanding or knowledge of what their grandparents went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my sister, my second oldest sister. Her name is Chaja, and her married name was Gaska, Gaska, G-A-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken, I think in Poland, after the war, early.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she here?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's-- she's passed away. And she passed away in 1991, 9th of August 1991. And she has left three children, and she has five grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photograph, she'd be probably in late 20's, probably. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my older sister. We called her Lola, but her name is Leah. She was-- her-- like, the family name was [? Elra ?] [INAUDIBLE] Rak and then she was married to her first husband. And he got killed in the war. And then she married. And she survived by herself, together with us in the forest, in the Bielski Otriad. And then, she came to Australia, and married her second husband, Mr. [? Shayag ?], [? Gedalia ?] [? Shayag. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is she in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- in this photograph, she's about 60.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my third sister, Franke. And she was-- stayed in Warsaw. She didn't live in '39. And she was-- she wasn't in the ghetto, but she was in the underground on-- as a-- as a Christian. She lived as-- on Aryan papers, they used to call it, yeah. And she worked for the underground organization. She was a messenger from the organization to the ghetto. She has to go backwards and forwards. And she lived through the-- almost to the liberation of Warsaw, when there was an uprising, a Polish uprising, not the Jewish, after the Jewish. It was on the other side. And she didn't make it. She got caught in the uprising, and shot. When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: This photograph was taken before the war, probably in 1938, something like that. How did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I obtained this photo-- my sister, Lola, after war she was living for a while in Paris. And she met one of my sister's-- my sister's old boyfriend, who survived the war in-- in-- in Russia, and came to live in Paris. And he carried her photo right through the-- through the war. And-- and then he met with my sister, and showed her the photo. And she asked for it, and he gave it to her. That's why. This is the only photo we have from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the original photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the original photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, myself, and my husband, my late husband. It was taken, I think in the-- in the early '70s.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [? Selabel, ?] or as he called himself here, Leon, which is the same thing. And he passed away. And we were married for 48 years. He was very, very important in the-- in the Otriad, in Bielski Otriad. He was a very good fighter. He-- he was in the Polish army, in artillery. And-- and he passed away in May 25, 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne. Went to-- I think we went to a-- a wedding, I think it was, all dressed up, of course. And then it was a photographer there, and he took the photograph. And then it didn't come out good. So I had another shot. And somehow it got my children. At this time, they weren't living at home. My daughter went overseas, and my son went to Canberra to study. So they all wanted a photograph to put on their shelves, on their desks. So we made this and send it to them. And that's how, you know, that's how we came to have this photo, by demand of the children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph taken from a book written about the Bielski Otriad, by-- by this man, here, in the middle. And he came to see us when we were in Italy, in a kibbutz of ex-partisans. And he needed a photograph of us to put in his book. He wrote about the-- the Bielski Otriad book in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a close up of the photograph, which we have just seen. Could you indicate where you are standing in the photograph, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't point my finger, can I?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Between the man in the glasses and the woman with the checked. Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is the writer of the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is sitting on the right side with-- in the white coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking? Those are the index cards. That-- that's when we first got registered with the refugees in Austria, Liegnitz. This was our only document we had after we left Poland. It served as-- as identification for refugees from Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have it from left to right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandson, Simon. He's 15. And that's my daughter-in-law, Monica. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the middle that's [INAUDIBLE], that's me. And then that's my grandson, Michael. He's now 18. And that's my daughter, Silvia, and I won't say how old she is. And at the moment, she is overseas, that's why she can't be here to give you a interview. But she is the one who was born in the Bielski Otriad, and survived and became a very accomplished young lady. I'm very proud of her. At the moment she is traveling, and teaching English overseas. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's my son, Sam, who couldn't have been included in the other photo, because he was taking the other-- with the other part of the family. And he was born, as I previously said, in Italy. He grew up in Australia. And he's now a practicing lawyer. I said it.
